q: A large mass M hangs stationary at the end of a light string that passes through a smooth fixed ring to a small mass m that moves around in a horizontal circular path. If l is the length of the string from m to the top end of the tube and $\theta$ is angle between this part and vertical part of the string as shown in the figure, then time taken by m to complete one circle is equal to
question: A large mass M hangs stationary at the end of a light string that passes through a smooth fixed ring to a small mass m that moves around in a horizontal circular path. If l is the length of the string from m to the top end of the tube and $\theta$ is angle between this part and vertical part of the string as shown in the figure, then time taken by m to complete one circle is equal to
solution:
The large mass M hangs suspended vertically at the lower section of the continuous, frictionless string. Since mass M is explicitly stated to remain completely stationary, its acceleration is zero.

Therefore, balancing the forces acting vertically on mass M:

Upward Tension $(T)$ = Downward Gravitational Force $(Mg)$

$$T = Mg {... (i)}$$

Because the tube is smooth and frictionless, the tension $T$ remains same throughout the entire length of the string.

The mass $m$ moves in a horizontal circle of radius $r$.

The tension force $T$ acting on mass $m$ into its horizontal and vertical components based on the angle $\theta$ it forms

with the vertical axis:

$$T\cos\theta = mg\ \ldots\text{(i)}$$

$$T\sin\theta = m\omega^2 r\ \ldots\text{(ii)}$$

Where $\omega$ is the constant angular velocity of mass $m$.

$$\sin\theta = \frac{r}{l} \Rightarrow r = l\sin\theta$$

From equation (ii):

$$T\sin\theta = m\omega^2 (l\sin\theta)$$

$$T = m\omega^2 l\ \ldots\text{(iii)}$$

Substituting the value of tension $T = Mg$ from equation (i)

$$Mg = m\omega^2 l$$

$$\omega^2 = \frac{Mg}{ml} \Rightarrow \omega = \sqrt{\frac{Mg}{ml}}$$

The time period required to complete one full revolution is related to the angular velocity by

$$T_{\text{period}} = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

$$T_{\text{period}} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{Mg}{ml}} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{ml}{gM}}$$

Therefore, the correct option is (D).

q: A Toy cart attached to the end of an unstretched string of length a, when revolved moves on a smooth horizontal table in a circle of radius 2a with a time period T. Now the toy cart is speeded up until it moves in a circle of radius 3a with a period T'. If Hook's law holds then (Assume no friction) :
question: A Toy cart attached to the end of an unstretched string of length a, when revolved moves on a smooth horizontal table in a circle of radius 2a with a time period T. Now the toy cart is speeded up until it moves in a circle of radius 3a with a period T'. If Hook's law holds then (Assume no friction) :
solution:
Hooke's law states that the restoring tension force $F$ developed within an elastic material is directly proportional to its linear elongation ($\Delta x$):

$$F = k \cdot \Delta x$$

where $k$ is the elastic force constant of the string.

$$\Delta x = \text{Current Radius } (r) - \text{Unstretched Natural Length } (a)$$

In the first case, the cart moves in a circle of radius $r_1 = 2a$.

The extension of the string is $\Delta x_1 = r_1 - a = 2a - a = a$

According to Hooke's law, the tension force $T_1$ is:

$$T_1 = k \cdot a$$

This tension force provides the necessary centripetal force for the cart of mass $m$ spinning with a time period $T$.

$$F_c = m \cdot \left(\frac{4\pi^2}{T^2}\right) \cdot r$$

$$k a = m .(\frac{4\pi^2}{T^2}) \cdot (2a)$$

$$k = \frac{8\pi^2 m}{T^2} \Rightarrow T^2 = \frac{8\pi^2 m}{k} \ldots (i)$$

In the second case, the cart is accelerated into an orbit of radius $r_2 = 3a$ with a new time period $T'$.

The new extension of the string is $\Delta x_2 = r_2 - a = 3a - a = 2a$

The new tension force $T_2$ is:

$$T_2 = k \cdot (2a)$$

$$k \cdot (2a) = m \cdot \left(\frac{4\pi^2}{T'^2}\right) \cdot (3a)$$

$$2k = \frac{12\pi^2 m}{T'^2}$$

$$T'^2 = \frac{12\pi^2 m}{2k} = \frac{6\pi^2 m}{k} \ldots (ii)$$

Dividing equation (ii) by equation (i)

$$\frac{T'^2}{T^2} = \frac{6\pi^2 m/k}{8\pi^2 m/k}$$

$$\frac{T'^2}{T^2} = \frac{6}{8} = \frac{3}{4}$$

$$\frac{T'}{T} = \sqrt{\frac{3}{4}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

$$T' = \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) T$$

Therefore, the correct option is (B)

q: A small object placed on a rotating horizontal turn table just slips when it is placed at a distance of 4 cm from the axis of rotation. If the angular velocity of the turn table is doubled, the object slips when its distance from the axis of rotation is :
question: A small object placed on a rotating horizontal turn table just slips when it is placed at a distance of 4 cm from the axis of rotation. If the angular velocity of the turn table is doubled, the object slips when its distance from the axis of rotation is :
solution:
Let an object of mass $m$ placed on a rough horizontal turntable at a distance $r$ from the centre of rotation. The turntable rotates with a constant angular velocity $\omega$.

From the perspective of an observer in the rotating reference frame of the turntable, the object experiences an outward centrifugal force $\left(\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{c}}\right)$.

$$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{m} \omega^2 \mathrm{r} $$

The horizontal force holding the object is the static friction force $\left(\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{s}}\right)$ exerted by the surface of the turntable. This force acts radially inward:

$$ \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{s}}=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{m} \omega^2 \mathrm{r} $$

As the rotation speed or distance increases, the required friction force increases. But static friction has a maximum limiting value ( $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{s}, \max }$ ) determined by the coefficient of static friction $\mu_{\mathrm{s}}$ and the normal reaction force N:

$$ \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{s}, \max }=\mu_{\mathrm{s}} \mathrm{~N} $$

Since the turntable is horizontal, the vertical forces gives $\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{mg}$. Thus, the maximum available frictional force is :

$$f_{s,\max} = \mu_s m g$$

The object is on the verge of slipping when the outward centrifugal force equals this maximum limiting static friction :

$$m\omega^2 r = \mu_s m g$$

$$\omega^2 r = \mu_s g$$

Since the object and the turntable surface remain the same, the coefficient of static friction $\mu_s$ and the acceleration due to gravity $g$ are constants. Therefore, the product $\omega^2 r$ must be a constant.

$$\omega_1^2 r_1 = \omega_2^2 r_2$$

$$r_2 = r_1 \left(\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}\right)^2$$

Initial distance from the centre is $r_1 = 4\ \text{cm}$

The angular velocity is doubled $\omega_2 = 2\omega_1$

$$r_2 = 4 \times \left(\frac{\omega_1}{2\omega_1}\right)^2$$

$$r_2 = 4 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2$$

$$r_2 = 4 \times \frac{1}{4}$$

$$r_2 = 1\ \text{cm}$$

Therefore, the new critical distance from the centre of turntable is $1\ \text{cm}$.

Thus, the correct option is (D).

q: A small stone is attached to one end of a string and rotated uniformly in a horizontal circular path of radius 80 cm. If it completes 14 revolutions in 25 s, the magnitude of its acceleration is
question: A small stone is attached to one end of a string and rotated uniformly in a horizontal circular path of radius 80 cm. If it completes 14 revolutions in 25 s, the magnitude of its acceleration is
solution:
For motion in a horizontal circle at constant speed, the acceleration is centripetal acceleration:

$$ a = \omega^2 r $$

Here,

$$ r = 80\ \text{cm} = 0.8\ \text{m} $$

The stone makes $14$ revolutions in $25\ \text{s}$, so its frequency is

$$ f = \frac{14}{25}\ \text{s}^{-1} $$

Angular speed is

$$ \omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \cdot \frac{14}{25} $$

Using $\pi = \frac{22}{7}$,

$$ \omega = 2 \cdot \frac{22}{7} \cdot \frac{14}{25} = \frac{88}{25} $$

So,

$$ a = \omega^2 r = \left(\frac{88}{25}\right)^2 \times 0.8 $$

$$ a = \frac{7744}{625} \times 0.8 $$

$$ a \approx 9.9\ \text{m s}^{-2} $$

This is closest to

$$ 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2} $$

Option C is correct.

q: A car travels on a straight road with a constant speed $v$. An observer $O$ is standing at a distance $r_0$ from the road. The angular velocity of the line $OP$ when the car is at the point $P(r, \theta)$ is given by
question: A car travels on a straight road with a constant speed $v$. An observer $O$ is standing at a distance $r_0$ from the road. The angular velocity of the line $OP$ when the car is at the point $P(r, \theta)$ is given by
solution:
Let a car move along a straight line path with a constant linear velocity vector $\vec{v}$.

An observer is at point O , at a perpendicular distance $\mathrm{r}_0$ from the line of motion.

Let P be the instantaneous position of the car at a distance r from O .

The angle between the horizontal line of reference and the position vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}$ is $\theta$.

$$\sin\theta = \frac{r_0}{r} \; \ldots \; (i)$$

The angular velocity $\omega$ of the line OP relative to the observer at O is determined by the component of linear velocity that is perpendicular ($\vec{v}_{\perp}$) to the line of sight OP:

$$\omega = \frac{v_{\perp}}{r}$$

Since the reference line at O is parallel to the straight road, the angle between the velocity vector $\vec{v}$ and the position line OP is equal to $\theta$.

Therefore, resolving $\vec{v}$ with respect to the line OP:

The component along the line OP is $v_{\parallel} = v\cos\theta$

The component perpendicular to the line OP is $v_{\perp} = v\sin\theta$

Substituting $v_{\perp} = v\sin\theta$ into angular velocity expression:

$$\omega = \frac{v\sin\theta}{r}$$

$$\omega = \frac{v\left(\dfrac{r_0}{r}\right)}{r}$$

$$\omega = \frac{r_0\,v}{r^2}$$

As the angle measured anti-clockwise is positive, so the angular displacement along clockwise rotation will be negative, $\vec{\omega}=-\frac{\mathrm{r}_0 \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{r}^2}$

Therefore, the expression for angular velocity is $-\frac{r_0 v}{r^2}$.

Thus, the correct option is (D).

q: Three point particles P, Q, R move in a circle of radius 'r' with different but constant speeds. They start moving at t = 0 from their initial positions as shown in the figure. The angular velocities (in rad/sec) of P, Q and R are $5\pi, 2\pi, 3\pi$ respectively, in the same sense. The time at which they all meet is :
question: Three point particles P, Q, R move in a circle of radius 'r' with different but constant speeds. They start moving at t = 0 from their initial positions as shown in the figure. The angular velocities (in rad/sec) of P, Q and R are $5\pi, 2\pi, 3\pi$ respectively, in the same sense. The time at which they all meet is :
solution:
Particles P, Q and R move in the same clockwise direction with constant angular velocities $\omega_P = 5\pi$, $\omega_Q = 2\pi$, and $\omega_R = 3\pi$:

The initial position of particle P is $\pi$ rad. At any instant of time $t$, its position with respect to positive x-axis as reference line will be,

$$\theta_P(t) = \pi - 5\pi t$$

The initial position of particle Q is $\frac{\pi}{2}$ rad. At any instant of time $t$, its position with respect to positive x-axis as reference line will be,

$$\theta_Q(t) = \frac{\pi}{2} - 2\pi t$$

The initial position of particle R is $0$ rad. At any instant of time $t$, its position with respect to positive x-axis as reference line will be,

$$\theta_R(t) = 0 - 3\pi t = -3\pi t$$

For any two moving particles to meet on a circular track, their angular position difference must be an integer multiple of a full revolution ($2\pi$ rad).

The time $t_1$ when particle P catches particle Q:

$$\theta_P(t) - \theta_Q(t) = 2n\pi \text{ (where n is an integer)}$$

$$\left(\pi - 5\pi t\right) - \left(\frac{\pi}{2} - 2\pi t\right) = 2 \times 0\pi$$

$$\frac{\pi}{2} - 3\pi t = 0 \Rightarrow t_{PQ} = \frac{1}{6}$$

The time $t_1$ when particle Q catches particle R:

$$\theta_Q(t) - \theta_R(t) = 2n\pi \text{ (where n is an integer)}$$

$$\frac{\pi}{2} - 2\pi t - (-3\pi t) = 0$$

$$t_2 = -\frac{1}{2}$$

As time can’t be negative, so $n = 1$,

$$\frac{\pi}{2} - 2\pi t - (-3\pi t) = 2\pi$$

$$\pi t = \frac{3\pi}{2} \Rightarrow t_{QR} = \frac{3}{2}$$

The time $t_3$ when particle P catches particle R for the first time;

$$\theta_P(t) - \theta_R(t) = 2n\pi \text{ (where n is an integer)}$$

$$\left(\pi - 5\pi t\right) - (-3\pi t) = 0$$

$$\pi = 2\pi t \Rightarrow t_{\mathrm{PR}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

So, $t_{\mathrm{PQ}} = \frac{1}{6}\,\mathrm{s}$, $t_{\mathrm{QR}} = \frac{3}{2}$, $t_{\mathrm{PR}} = \frac{1}{2}$

All three particles be meeting from an initial alignment at an interval related to the least common multiple

(LCM) of these relative rotation times :

$$ \operatorname{LCM}\left(\frac{1}{6}, \frac{3}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{\operatorname{LCM}(1,3,1)}{\operatorname{HCF}(6,2,2)}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{~s} $$

Therefore, the correct option is (D).

subject: Physics
q: The velocity and acceleration vectors of a particle undergoing circular motion are $\vec{v} = 2 \hat{i} \text{ m/s}$ and $\vec{a} = 2\hat{i} + 4\hat{j} \text{ m/s}^2$ respectively at an instant of time. The radius of the circle is
question: The velocity and acceleration vectors of a particle undergoing circular motion are $\vec{v} = 2 \hat{i} \text{ m/s}$ and $\vec{a} = 2\hat{i} + 4\hat{j} \text{ m/s}^2$ respectively at an instant of time. The radius of the circle is
solution:
The velocity vector of a particle is given as $\vec{v} = 2\hat{i}\ \mathrm{m/s}$

The instantaneous speed $v$ of the particle is the magnitude of the velocity vector:

$$v = |\vec{v}| = 2\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

In circular motion, the velocity vector is always directed tangentially to the circular track. Therefore, at any instant, the tangential direction lies along the positive x-axis $(\hat{i})$.

The total acceleration vector is given as:

$$\vec{a} = 2\hat{i} + 4\hat{j}\ \mathrm{m/s}^2$$

Acceleration vector $\vec{a}$ in two-dimensional circular motion can be written as the sum of its components along the tangential direction ($\hat{u}_t$) and the normal/centripetal direction ($\hat{u}_c$):

$$\vec{a} = \vec{a}_t + \vec{a}_c$$

As the tangential direction is along $\hat{i}$, any component of acceleration along $\hat{i}$ belongs to the tangential acceleration ($\vec{a}_t$), and the component perpendicular to it along $\hat{j}$ must be the centripetal acceleration ($\vec{a}_c$).

Tangential component of acceleration is $\vec{a}_t = 2\hat{i}\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$

Centripetal/Normal component of acceleration is $\vec{a}_c = 4\hat{j}\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$

The magnitude of the centripetal acceleration vector is $a_c = |\vec{a}_c| = 4\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$

Also, the magnitude of centripetal acceleration ($a_c$) in terms of instantaneous speed ($v$) and radius ($R$) is

$$a_c = \frac{v^2}{R}$$

Using $v = 2 { m/s}$ and $a_c = 4 { m/s}^2$:

$$R = \frac{v^2}{a_c}$$

$$R = \frac{(2)^2}{4} = \frac{4}{4} = 1\text{ m}$$

Therefore, the radius of circle is 1 m. Thus, the correct option is (A).

subject: Physics
q: A particle A moves along a circle of radius R = 50 cm so that its radius vector r relative to the fixed point O (Figure) rotates with the constant angular velocity $\omega = 0.40$ rad/s. Then modulus v of the velocity of the particle, and the modulus a of its total acceleration will be
question: A particle A moves along a circle of radius R = 50 cm so that its radius vector r relative to the fixed point O (Figure) rotates with the constant angular velocity $\omega = 0.40$ rad/s. Then modulus v of the velocity of the particle, and the modulus a of its total acceleration will be
solution:
Let the fixed point O lie on the circumference of the circle of radius $R$.

Let C be the centre of the circle.

The particle A moves along the circumference. The line segment OA is the radius vector $\vec{r}$ of the particle with respect to the fixed point O.

Let $\theta$ be the angular position of the particle measured from the centre C relative to the diameter line passing through O.

In triangle $\Delta OCA$:

$OC = CA = R$ (both are radii of the circle).

Since $\Delta OCA$ is an isosceles triangle, the angles opposite to the equal sides are equal $\angle COA = \angle CAO$.

The central angle $\angle SCA$ is equal to the sum of the interior alternate angles:

$$\theta = \angle COA + \angle CAO = 2 \cdot \angle COA$$

Let $\phi = \angle COA$ be the angle that the radius vector $\vec{r}$ makes with the fixed reference axis through $O$. Therefore:

$$\theta = 2\phi$$

Differentiating this with respect to time $t$ gives the relation between the angular velocity about the centre $\left(\omega_C = \frac{d\theta}{dt}\right)$ and the given angular velocity about point $O$ $\left(\omega = \frac{d\phi}{dt} = 0.40\ \text{rad/s}\right)$:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dt} = 2\frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

$$\omega_C = 2\omega$$

$$\omega_C = 2 \times 0.40\ \text{rad/s} = 0.80\ \text{rad/s}$$

The linear speed $v$ of a particle moving in a circle is determined by its angular velocity relative to the centre of the circle $\left(\omega_C\right)$

$$v = \omega_C \cdot R$$

The radius is given as $R = 50\ \text{cm} = 0.5\ \text{m}$

$$v = 0.80 \times 0.5\ \text{m/s} = 0.40\ \text{m/s}$$

Since the angular velocity $\omega$ with respect to the fixed point $O$ is constant, the angular velocity about the centre $\omega_C = 2\omega$ is also constant.

Because $\omega_C$ is constant, the linear speed $v = \omega_C R$ remains constant over time. This means the tangential acceleration component is zero:

$$a_t = \frac{dv}{dt} = 0$$

Therefore, the total acceleration consists entirely of the centripetal (normal) acceleration component:

$$a = a_n = \frac{v^2}{R}$$

$$a = \frac{(0.40)^2}{0.5} = \frac{0.16}{0.5} = 0.32\ \text{m/s}^2$$

Therefore, the correct option is (D).

q: The figure shows the velocity and acceleration of a point like body at the initial moment of its motion. The acceleration vector of the body remains constant. The minimum radius of curvature of trajectory of the body is
question: The figure shows the velocity and acceleration of a point like body at the initial moment of its motion. The acceleration vector of the body remains constant. The minimum radius of curvature of trajectory of the body is
solution:
The radius of curvature $R$ of a particle's trajectory at any given point is related to its instantaneous speed $v$ and

its component of acceleration perpendicular to the velocity vector (normal or centripetal acceleration, $a_n$),

$$R = \frac{v^2}{a_n}$$

The body is given an initial velocity $v_0 = 8\ \mathrm{m/s}$ and experiences a constant acceleration $a = 2\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$ directed

at an angle of $150^\circ$ relative to the velocity vector.

Parallel component of acceleration along velocity is,

$$a_{\parallel} = a\cos(150^\circ) = 2 \times \left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = -\sqrt{3}\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$$

Since this component is negative, it acts as a decelerating force, slowing the particle down.

Perpendicular component ($a_{\perp}$) to the velocity vector is,

$$a_{\perp} = a\sin(150^\circ) = 2 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = 1\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$$

The velocity decreases to a minimum value when the component of velocity parallel to the acceleration vector becomes zero similar to projectile motion. At this turning point, the velocity vector is perpendicular to the total constant acceleration vector $\vec{a}$.

The component of velocity perpendicular to the acceleration vector is:

$$v_{\perp} = v_0 \sin(180^\circ - 150^\circ) = v_0 \sin(30^\circ)$$

$$v_{\perp} = 8 \times \frac{1}{2} = 4\,\mathrm{m/s}$$

Therefore, the velocity component $v_{\perp}$ remains strictly constant throughout the entire motion

$$v_{\min} = v_{\perp} = 4\,\mathrm{m/s}$$

At turning point, the instantaneous net speed reaches its minimum possible value $v = 4\,\mathrm{m/s}$.

The constant acceleration vector $\vec{a}$ acts perpendicular to this velocity. Thus, the normal acceleration is equal to the total acceleration $a_n = a = 2\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$.

So, using radius of curvature formula the minimum radius of curvature is,

$$R_{\min} = \frac{v_{\min}^2}{a} = \frac{4^2}{2} = \frac{16}{2} = 8\,\mathrm{m}$$

Therefore, the correct option is (C).

subject: Physics
q: A particle is projected horizontally from the top of a tower with a velocity $v_0$. If $v$ be its velocity at any instant, then the radius of curvature of the path of the particle at that instant is directly proportional to :
question: A particle is projected horizontally from the top of a tower with a velocity $v_0$. If $v$ be its velocity at any instant, then the radius of curvature of the path of the particle at that instant is directly proportional to :
solution:
When a particle is launched horizontally with an initial speed $\mathrm{v}_0$ from the top of a tower (origin $(0,0)$ )

Horizontal component of velocity ( $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}}$ ) stays constant because there is no horizontal acceleration :

$$ \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{v}_0 $$

Vertical component of velocity ($v_y$) increases over time due to the acceleration of gravity $g$ acting downwards:

$$v_y = gt$$

At any given instant, the net instantaneous velocity magnitude $v$ is

$$v = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2} \rightarrow v^2 = v_0^2 + v_y^2$$

Let $\phi$ be the angle that the instantaneous velocity vector $\vec{v}$ makes with the horizontal axis at that moment:

$$\cos \phi = \frac{v_x}{v} = \frac{v_0}{v}$$

The projection of gravity vector perpendicular to the instantaneous velocity line is the normal acceleration.

$$a_n = g \cos \phi$$

Substituting the expression for $\cos \phi = \frac{v_0}{v}$

$$a_n = g\left(\frac{v_0}{v}\right)$$

The relationship for the radius of curvature $R$ is:

$$R = \frac{v^2}{a_n}$$

$$R = \frac{v^2}{g\left(\frac{v_0}{v}\right)}$$

$$R = \frac{v^3}{g \cdot v_0}$$

Since the gravitational acceleration $g$ and the initial horizontal launch velocity $v_0$ are constants

$$R \propto v^3$$

Therefore, the correct option is (A).

subject: Physics
q:
A particle moves with deceleration along the circle of radius $R$ so that at any moment of time its tangential and normal accelerations are equal in magnitude. At the initial moment $t = 0$ the speed of the particle equals $v_0$, then :

The total acceleration of the particle as function of velocity.
question:
A particle moves with deceleration along the circle of radius $R$ so that at any moment of time its tangential and normal accelerations are equal in magnitude. At the initial moment $t = 0$ the speed of the particle equals $v_0$, then :

The total acceleration of the particle as function of velocity.
solution:
The total acceleration vector $\vec{a}$ is composed of two mutually perpendicular vectors the tangential acceleration $\vec{a}_t$ and the normal acceleration $\vec{a}_n$.

$$a = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_n^2}$$

We are given that the magnitudes of the tangential and normal accelerations are equal everywhere :

$$|a_t| = a_n = \frac{v^2}{R}$$

From the total acceleration formula :

$$a = \sqrt{\left(\frac{v^2}{R}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{v^2}{R}\right)^2}$$

$$a = \sqrt{2}\left(\frac{v^2}{R}\right)^2$$

$$a = \frac{\sqrt{2}v^2}{R}$$

Therefore, the correct options is (A)

q: Two particles of mass m1 and m2 are fastened respectively to the middle point and one end of a massless string which is laid on a smooth horizontal table, the other end being kept fixed. The particles are projected so that they describe circles with uniform speed such that the portions of string remain in a straight line. The ratio of tension in the part of the string from fixed point to m1 and that in the part of the string from m1 to the m2 will be:
question: Two particles of mass m1 and m2 are fastened respectively to the middle point and one end of a massless string which is laid on a smooth horizontal table, the other end being kept fixed. The particles are projected so that they describe circles with uniform speed such that the portions of string remain in a straight line. The ratio of tension in the part of the string from fixed point to m1 and that in the part of the string from m1 to the m2 will be:
solution:
Let the fixed end of the string be point O .

The total length of the string is L .

The first mass, $\mathrm{m}_1$, is fastened to the middle point of the string. Therefore, its distance from the fixed point O (its orbital radius $\mathrm{R}_1$ ) is:

$$ \mathrm{R}_1=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2} $$

The second mass, $\mathrm{m}_2$, is attached to the very end of the string. Its distance from the fixed point O (its orbital radius $R_2$ ) is:

$$ \mathrm{R}_2=\mathrm{L} $$

The portions of the string remain in a straight line during rotation. This means that as the string rotates about the fixed point O, both masses sweep out the same angle $\theta$ in any given time interval $t$.

The angular velocity is $\omega = \frac{\mathrm{d}\theta}{\mathrm{d}t}$. Because they rotate inline, both masses have the same angular velocity.

$$\omega_1 = \omega_2 = \omega$$

Let $T_1$ be the tension in the inner segment of the string from the fixed point O to $m_1$ and $T_2$ be the tension in the outer segment of the string from $m_1$ to $m_2$.

The only horizontal force acting on $m_2$ is the tension $T_2$ pulling it inward toward the centre. This net force provides the centripetal acceleration $a_{c2} = \omega^2 R_2$:

$$T_2 = m_2\,\omega^2 R_2$$

$$T_2 = m_2\,\omega^2 L \ldots \text{(i)}$$

Two horizontal forces act on $m_1$, are the inner string pulls it inward with tension $T_1$, while the outer string pulls it outward with tension $T_2$. The net inward force provides its centripetal acceleration $a_{c1} = \omega^2 R_1$:

$$T_1 - T_2 = m_1\,\omega^2 R_1$$

Substituting $R_1 = \frac{L}{2}$:

$$T_1 - T_2 = m_1\,\omega^2 \left(\frac{L}{2}\right)$$

$$T_1 = T_2 + m_1\,\omega^2 \left(\frac{L}{2}\right) \ldots \text{(ii)}$$

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{T_2 + m_1\,\omega^2\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)}{T_2} = 1 + \frac{m_1\,\omega^2\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)}{T_2}$$

From equation (i),

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = 1 + \frac{m_1\,\omega^2\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)}{m_2\,\omega^2\,L}$$

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = 1 + \frac{\left(\dfrac{m_1}{2}\right)}{m_2} = 1 + \frac{m_1}{2m_2}$$

Therefore, the ratio of tensions is $1 + \dfrac{m_1}{2m_2}$. Thus, the correct option is (A).

subject: Physics
q: If a particle is projected with speed $u$ from ground at an angle $\theta$ with horizontal, then radius of curvature of a point where velocity vector is perpendicular to initial velocity vector is given by :
question: If a particle is projected with speed $u$ from ground at an angle $\theta$ with horizontal, then radius of curvature of a point where velocity vector is perpendicular to initial velocity vector is given by :
solution:
Let the launch point be the origin.

The initial velocity vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}}$ in Cartesian vector form is,

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}}=(\mathrm{u} \cos \theta) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(\mathrm{u} \sin \theta) \hat{\mathrm{j}} $$

In projectile motion, there is no acceleration in the horizontal direction ( $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{x}}=0$ ), so the horizontal velocity component remains constant throughout the flight:

$$ \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{u} \cos \theta $$

Gravity acts downwards, providing a constant vertical deceleration $\left(\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{y}}=-\mathrm{g}\right)$.

The vertical velocity component at any time t is:

$$ \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{u} \sin \theta-\mathrm{gt} $$

Thus, the instantaneous velocity vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ at any point along the trajectory is:

$$ \vec{v}=(u \cos \theta) \hat{\imath}+v_y \hat{\jmath} $$

The instantaneous velocity vector $\vec{v}$ becomes perpendicular to the initial velocity vector $\vec{u}$ at any time $t$. As the dot product of two perpendicular vectors is zero

$$\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

$$[(u\cos\theta)\hat{i} + (u\sin\theta)\hat{j}] \cdot [(u\cos\theta)\hat{i} + v_y] = 0$$

$$u^2\cos^2\theta + u\sin\theta\,(v_y) = 0$$

$$u\sin\theta\,(v_y) = -u^2\cos^2\theta$$

$$v_y = \frac{-u^2\cos^2\theta}{u\sin\theta} = -u\cot\theta\cos\theta$$

The magnitude of the instantaneous linear speed $v$ is:

$$v = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2}$$

Substituting $v_x = u\cos\theta$ and $v_y = -u\cot\theta\cos\theta$:

$$v = \sqrt{(u\cos\theta)^2 + (-u\cot\theta\cos\theta)^2}$$

$$v = \sqrt{u^2\cos^2\theta + u^2\cot^2\theta\cos^2\theta}$$

$$v = \sqrt{u^2\cos^2\theta\,(1 + \cot^2\theta)}$$

Using the trigonometric identity

$$1 + \cot^2\theta = \csc^2\theta = \frac{1}{\sin^2\theta}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{u^2\cos^2\theta}{\sin^2\theta}} = \sqrt{u^2\cot^2\theta} = u\cot\theta$$

For any curved motion path, the normal (centripetal) component of acceleration $a_n$ is related to the instantaneous speed $v$ and local radius of curvature $R$ by the relation:

$$a_n = \frac{v^2}{R} \Rightarrow R = \frac{v^2}{a_n} \ldots (i)$$

Here, the total acceleration acting on the projectile is gravity $(g)$, pointing downwards.

Let $\alpha$ be the angle that the velocity vector $\vec{v}$ makes with the horizontal line at this moment.

The component of gravity perpendicular to the velocity path is:

$$a_n = g\cos\alpha$$

Using the vector components:

$$\cos\alpha = \frac{v_x}{v} = \frac{u\cos\theta}{u\cot\theta} = \frac{\cos\theta}{\left(\frac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta}\right)} = \sin\theta \ldots (ii)$$

Therefore, the normal acceleration component at this point is:

$$a_n = g\sin\theta \ldots (iii)$$

From equation (i), (ii) and (iii),

$$R = \frac{(u\cot\theta)^2}{g\sin\theta} = \frac{u^2\cot^2\theta}{g\sin\theta}$$

Therefore, the correct option is (B).

subject: Physics
q: An object moves counter-clockwise along the circular path shown below. As it moves along the path its acceleration vector continuously points towards point S. The object :
question: An object moves counter-clockwise along the circular path shown below. As it moves along the path its acceleration vector continuously points towards point S. The object :
options: speeds up at P, Q and R
slows down at P, Q and R
speeds up at P and slows down at R
slows down at P and speeds up at R
solution:
When an object moves along a circular path, its total acceleration vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}$ is made of centripetal (radial) acceleration ( $\vec{a}_c$ ) towards the centre and tangential acceleration ( $\vec{a}_t$ ) along the tangent to the circular path.

Let $\theta$ be the angle between the total acceleration vector $\vec{a}$ and the velocity vector $\vec{v}$.

The tangential component is given by $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{t}}=\mathrm{a} \cos \theta$.

The tangential acceleration is the rate of change of speed,

$$ a_t=\frac{d v}{d t} $$

If $\vec{a}$ makes an acute angle with $\vec{v}(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{v}>0)$, then $a_t>0$, which means the object speeds up.

If $\vec{a}$ makes an obtuse angle with $\vec{v}(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{v}<0)$, then $a_t<0$, which means the object slows down.

At the topmost point P , the object is moving counter-clockwise, so its velocity vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{P}}$ points horizontally to the left.

The angle between the velocity vector $\vec{v}_P$ and the acceleration vector $\vec{a}_P$ is acute $\left(\theta<90^{\circ}\right)$.

Because the component of acceleration along the tangent aligns with the direction of motion, the object speeds up at P .

At the middle point Q , the object moving counter-clockwise has a velocity vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{Q}}$ pointing vertically downward.

The angle between the velocity vector $\vec{v}_R$ and the acceleration vector $\vec{a}_R$ is right angle ( $\theta=90^{\circ}$ ).

Because the component of acceleration along the radius, the object is at constant speed at Q .

At the bottommost point R , the object moving counter-clockwise has a velocity vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{R}}$ pointing horizontally to the right.

The angle between the velocity vector $\vec{v}_R$ and the acceleration vector $\vec{a}_R$ is obtuse ( $\theta>90^{\circ}$ ).

Because the component of acceleration along the tangent opposes the direction of motion, the object slows down at R .

Therefore, the object speeds up at P and shows down at R . Thus, the correct option is (C) .

subject: Physics
q: The diagram shows a CD rotating clockwise (as seen from above) in the CD-player. After turning it off, the CD slows down. Assuming it has not come to a stop yet, the direction of the acceleration of point P at this instance is :
question: The diagram shows a CD rotating clockwise (as seen from above) in the CD-player. After turning it off, the CD slows down. Assuming it has not come to a stop yet, the direction of the acceleration of point P at this instance is :
solution:
The CD is rotating clockwise when viewed from above.

The point P is on the leftmost edge of the disc. In a clockwise rotation particle on the left edge move upward.

Therefore, the instantaneous linear velocity vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ of point P points vertically upward.

Any point undergoing circular motion experiences a centripetal acceleration component directed toward the centre of rotation to sustain its curved path.

Thus, the centripetal acceleration vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{\mathrm{c}}$ points horizontally to the right.

It is given that after turning the CD player off, the disc slows down.

For an object slowing down, its tangential acceleration component must act directly opposite to its instantaneous velocity vector $\left(\vec{a}_t \downarrow \uparrow \vec{v}\right)$.

Since the velocity vector $\vec{v}$ at point $P$ points vertically upward, the tangential acceleration vector $\vec{a}_t$ must point vertically downward.

The net acceleration vector is the vector sum of these two perpendicular components:

$$ \vec{a}_{n e t}=\vec{a}_c+\vec{a}_t $$

So, the net acceleration vector be along diagonal down and to the right ( $4^{\text {th }}$ quadrant).

Therefore, the correct option is (A).

subject: Physics
q:
Statement-1: In case of uniform circular motion acceleration is constant.

Statement-2: In uniform circular motion, angular acceleration is constant.
question:
Statement-1: In case of uniform circular motion acceleration is constant.

Statement-2: In uniform circular motion, angular acceleration is constant.
options: Statement-1 is true, statement-2 is true and statement-2 is correct explanation for statement-1
Statement-1 is true, statement-2 is true and statement-2 is not the correct explanation for statement-1
solution:
Let a particle is executing uniform circular motion in a counter-clockwise direction.

The position vector $\vec{r}$ of the particle moving in a circle of radius $R$ with a constant angular speed $\omega$ can be written as:

$$\vec{r} = R\cos(\omega t)\,\hat{i} + R\sin(\omega t)\,\hat{j}$$

Differentiating the position vector with respect to time gives the velocity vector $\vec{v}$:

$$\vec{v} = \frac{d\vec{r}}{dt} = -R\omega\sin(\omega t)\,\hat{i} + R\omega\cos(\omega t)\,\hat{j}$$

Differentiating the velocity vector with respect to time gives the acceleration vector $\vec{a}$:

$$\vec{a} = \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = -R\omega^2\cos(\omega t)\,\hat{i} - R\omega^2\sin(\omega t)\,\hat{j} = -\omega^2\vec{r}$$

So, the magnitude of this acceleration vector is:

$$|\vec{a}| = \sqrt{\left(-R\omega^2\cos(\omega t)\right)^2 + \left(-R\omega^2\sin(\omega t)\right)^2}$$

$$|\vec{a}| = \omega^2 R\sqrt{\cos^2(\omega t) + \sin^2(\omega t)} = \omega^2 R$$

The magnitude ($\omega^2 R$) remains constant throughout the motion, its direction ($-\vec{r}$) points toward the centre of the circle at every single instant. As the particle moves along the circular path, this direction continuously changes. Because acceleration is a vector quantity, a change in direction means the acceleration vector itself is not constant.

Therefore, Statement-1 is false.

In uniform circular motion, the linear speed v of the particle is constant. The relationship between linear speed and angular velocity $\omega$ is:

$$ \mathrm{v}=\mathrm{R} \omega \Rightarrow \omega=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{R}} $$

Since both v and R are constants, the angular velocity $\omega$ is also constant.

Angular acceleration ( $\alpha$ ) is the time rate of change of angular velocity:

$$ \alpha=\frac{\mathrm{d} \omega}{\mathrm{dt}} $$

Since $\omega$ does not change with time, its derivative is zero:

$$ \alpha=0 $$

Thus, the angular acceleration remains constant at zero throughout the entire motion.

Therefore, Statement-1 is false; statement-2 is true. Hence, the correct option is (D).

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving in a circle :
question: A particle is moving in a circle :
options: The resultant force on the particle must be towards the centre
The resultant force may be towards the centre
The direction of the angular acceleration and the angular velocity must be the same
The cross product of the tangential acceleration and the angular velocity will be zero.
solution:
For a particle of mass $m$ executing non-uniform circular motion, the net acceleration vector $\vec{a}$ is composed of two perpendicular components :

$$ \vec{a}=\vec{a}_c+\vec{a}_t $$

Where $\vec{a}_c$ is the centripetal acceleration vector ( $a_c=\frac{v^2}{R}$, pointing toward the centre) and $\vec{a}_t$ is the tangential acceleration vector $\left(a_t=\frac{d v}{d t}\right.$, pointing along the tangent).

According to Newton's Second Law $\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\text {net }}=\mathrm{m} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}\right)$ :

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{net}}=\mathrm{m} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{\mathrm{c}}+\mathrm{m} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{\mathrm{t}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{c}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{t}} $$

If the particle is in non-uniform circular motion, its speed varies over time, $\Rightarrow a_t \neq 0$.

Consequently, a non-zero tangential force $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{t}}$ exists, and the total resultant force $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{net}}$ will point at an angle to the centre rather than toward it. Thus, the resultant force does not always have to be directed towards the centre. Hence, the statement (a) is incorrect.

If the particle is in uniform circular motion, its speed is constant, meaning $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{t}}=0$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{t}}=0$. In this case, the net force consists of the centripetal force $\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{net}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{c}}\right)$, which points toward the centre. Because circular motion can be uniform, the resultant force may be directed towards the centre. Thus, the statement (b) correct.

Angular velocity ( $\vec{\omega}$ ) and angular acceleration ( $\vec{\alpha}$ ) are axial vectors along the axis of rotation.

When a particle is speeding up in its rotation, $\vec{\alpha}$ points in the same direction as $\vec{\omega}$.

When a particle is slowing down in its rotation, $\vec{\alpha}$ points in the opposite direction to $\vec{\omega}$ to create an angular deceleration.

Because they point in opposite directions during deceleration, so statement (c) is incorrect.

Let the circular motion occur in the xy-plane. The angular velocity vector $\vec{\omega}$ acts perpendicular to the plane of motion, pointing along the z -axis :

$\vec{\omega}=\omega \hat{\mathrm{k}}$

The tangential acceleration vector $\vec{a}_t$ is tangential to the path so it will always be within the plane of motion (xy-plane).

$$\vec{a}_t = a_{tx}\hat{i} + a_{ty}\hat{j}$$

The cross product $\vec{a}_t \times \vec{\omega}$:

$$\vec{a}_t \times \vec{\omega} = (a_{tx}\hat{i} + a_{ty}\hat{j}) \times (\omega\hat{k})$$

$$\vec{a}_t \times \vec{\omega} = -a_{tx}\omega\hat{j} + a_{ty}\omega\hat{i}$$

For non-zero values of velocity and acceleration components, this cross-product is non-zero because the vectors are mutually perpendicular, so statement (d) is incorrect.

Therefore, the correct option is (B).

subject: Physics
q: A car is moving due east with constant speed. It takes a left turn as shown. At point A :
question: A car is moving due east with constant speed. It takes a left turn as shown. At point A :
options: the velocity of the car is northward and acceleration is also due north.
the velocity of the car is southward and acceleration is due east.
the velocity of car is northward and acceleration is due west.
the velocity of car is southward and acceleration is due west.
solution:
The car begins its path traveling due East $(+\hat{1})$ and takes left turn to point towards the North $(+\hat{\jmath})$.

At point A, which is located on this turning arc, the car's direction of motion has changed. The instantaneous velocity vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}$ of any particle is always directed along the tangent to its path of motion.

So, the tangent vector at the point A is along northward.

The car is in circular motion with a constant speed. Tangential acceleration $\left(a_t\right)$ is the rate of change of speed $\left(\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{t}}=\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)$.

$$ a_t=0 $$

The car is in circular arc of radius R , it experiences a centripetal acceleration component to change its direction:

$$ \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{R}} $$

The centripetal acceleration vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{\mathrm{c}}$ points perpendicular to the velocity, inward toward the centre of the circular arc. Therefore, at point A, the centripetal acceleration vector points due West.

Hence, the velocity direction is Northward and acceleration direction is Westward. Thus, the correct option is (C).

subject: Physics
q:
Statement-1 : Angular velocity of body A with respect to another B is always given $ \vec{\omega}_A - \vec{\omega}_B = \vec{\omega}_{AB} $. Where $ \vec{\omega}_A $ and $ \vec{\omega}_B $ are angular velocities of the two rotating bodies.

Statement-2 : Velocity of body A with respect to another B is $ \vec{v}_A - \vec{v}_B $.
question:
Statement-1 : Angular velocity of body A with respect to another B is always given $ \vec{\omega}_A - \vec{\omega}_B = \vec{\omega}_{AB} $. Where $ \vec{\omega}_A $ and $ \vec{\omega}_B $ are angular velocities of the two rotating bodies.

Statement-2 : Velocity of body A with respect to another B is $ \vec{v}_A - \vec{v}_B $.
options: Statement-1 is true, statement-2 is true and statement-2 is correct explanation for statement-1
Statement-1 is true, statement-2 is true and statement-2 is not the correct explanation for statement-1
solution:
The relative velocity of a particle $A$ with respect to a particle $B\left(\vec{v}_{A B}\right)$ is the rate of change of the relative position vector of $A$ with respect to $B\left(\vec{r}_{A B}\right)$.

Let $\vec{r}_A$ and $\vec{r}_B$ be the position vectors of particles $A$ and $B$ relative to a fixed origin in an inertial frame.

The relative position vector is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{AB}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{A}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{B}}$

Differentiating this position vector with respect to time t :

$$\frac{d\vec{r}_{AB}}{dt}=\frac{d\vec{r}_A}{dt}-\frac{d\vec{r}_B}{dt}$$

$$\vec{v}_{AB}=\vec{v}_A-\vec{v}_B$$

Therefore, Statement-2 is true.

The angular velocity of a particle A as observed by an observer at B ($\vec{\omega}_{AB}$) depends on their relative position vector $\vec{r}_{AB}$ and their relative velocity vector $\vec{v}_{AB}$.

The angular velocity $\omega$ of a particle about a point with separation distance $r$ is

$$\omega=\frac{V_{\perp}}{r}$$

The component of its velocity perpendicular to the line joining them is ($V_{\perp}$).

So, the relative linear velocity and relative angular velocity is given by the cross-product equation:

$$\vec{v}_{AB}=\vec{\omega}_{AB}\times\vec{r}_{AB}$$

If body A and body B are rotating about two different axes of rotation, their individual angular velocities $\vec{\omega}_{A1}$ and $\vec{\omega}_{B2}$ are relative to two different reference line 1 and 2.

Le body A is rotating about the reference line 1 and body B is rotating about the reference line 2.

$$\vec{\omega}_{A1}=\vec{\omega}_A-\vec{\omega}_1$$

$$\vec{\omega}_{B2}=\vec{\omega}_B-\vec{\omega}_2$$

So, the relative angular velocity of A with relative to B is $\vec{\omega}_{AB}=\vec{\omega}_A-\vec{\omega}_B$

$$\vec{\omega}_{AB}=(\vec{\omega}_{A1}+\vec{\omega}_1)-(\vec{\omega}_{B2}+\vec{\omega}_2)$$

$$\vec{\omega}_{AB}=(\vec{\omega}_{A1}+\vec{\omega}_{B2})-(\vec{\omega}_1-\vec{\omega}_2)$$

So, the expression $\vec{\omega}_{AB}=\vec{\omega}_A-\vec{\omega}_B$ is valid only if the two particles are rotating in the same frame of reference. Hence, the statement-1 is false.

So, statement-1 is false and statement-2 is true. Thus, the correct option is (D).

subject: Physics
q: A particle is projected at angle 53° to the horizontal at speed of 10 m/s. Find its tangential acceleration at $t = 1.4$ sec.
question: A particle is projected at angle 53° to the horizontal at speed of 10 m/s. Find its tangential acceleration at $t = 1.4$ sec.
solution:
Let the horizontal direction be along the positive x-axis ($\hat{i}$) and the vertical direction be along the positive y-axis ($\hat{j}$).

A particle projected with an initial speed $u$ at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal has initial velocity components given by:

$$\vec{u} = (u \cos \theta)\hat{i} + (u \sin \theta)\hat{j}$$

The initial speed of throw is given as $u = 10\ \text{m/s}$ and $\theta = 53^{\circ}$:

$$\vec{u} = (10 \times \cos 53^{\circ})\hat{i} + (10 \times \sin 53^{\circ})\hat{j} = 6\hat{i} + 8\hat{j}$$

During projectile motion, there is no acceleration in the horizontal direction ($a_x = 0$), so the horizontal velocity component remains constant:

$$v_x = u_x = 6\ \text{m/s}$$

The only acceleration acting on the particle is gravity, which acts vertically downwards ($a_y = -g = -10\ \text{m/s}^2$).

Using the first equation of motion for the vertical component $v_y = u_y + a_y t$:

$$v_y = 8 - 10t$$

At the given time $t = 1.4\ \text{sec}$:

$$v_y = 8 - 10(1.4) = 8 - 14 = -6\ \text{m/s}$$

Thus, the instantaneous velocity vector $\vec{v}$ at $t = 1.4\ \text{sec}$ is:

$$\vec{v} = 6\hat{i} - 6\hat{j}$$

The magnitude of velocity (speed $v$):

$$v = \sqrt{6^2 + (-6)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 36} = \sqrt{72} = 6\sqrt{2}\ \text{m/s}$$

Tangential acceleration is defined as the rate of change of linear speed with respect to time ($a_t = \frac{dv}{dt}$). The speed $v$ is the magnitude of the velocity vector:

$$v = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2}$$

$$v^2 = v_x^2 + v_y^2$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to time $t$:

$$2v\,\frac{dv}{dt} = 2v_x\,\frac{dv_x}{dt} + 2v_y\,\frac{dv_y}{dt}$$

$$v\,\frac{dv}{dt} = v_x\,a_x + v_y\,a_y$$

Since $a_t = \frac{dv}{dt}$,

$$v\,a_t = \vec{v} \cdot \vec{a} \Rightarrow a_t = \frac{\vec{v} \cdot \vec{a}}{v}$$

The total acceleration vector for a projectile is due to gravity:

$$\vec{a} = 0\hat{i} - 10\hat{j}$$

So, the tangential acceleration is,

$$a_t = \frac{\vec{v} \cdot \vec{a}}{v} = \frac{(6)(0) + (-6)(-10)}{6\sqrt{2}} = \frac{0 + 60}{6\sqrt{2}}\,\text{m/s}^2$$

$$a_t = \frac{10}{\sqrt{2}}\,\text{m/s}^2 = 5\sqrt{2}\,\text{m/s}^2$$

Therefore, the correct option is (B).

subject: Physics
q: A solid body rotates with deceleration about a stationary axis with an angular deceleration $\boldsymbol{\beta} \boldsymbol{\alpha} \sqrt{\boldsymbol{\omega}}$ where $\omega$ is its angular velocity. If at the initial moment of time its angular velocity was equal to $\omega_0$ then the mean angular velocity of the body averaged over the whole time of rotation till it comes to rest is $\omega_0 / \mathrm{n}$ where n is.
question: A solid body rotates with deceleration about a stationary axis with an angular deceleration $\boldsymbol{\beta} \boldsymbol{\alpha} \sqrt{\boldsymbol{\omega}}$ where $\omega$ is its angular velocity. If at the initial moment of time its angular velocity was equal to $\omega_0$ then the mean angular velocity of the body averaged over the whole time of rotation till it comes to rest is $\omega_0 / \mathrm{n}$ where n is.
solution:
The angular deceleration $\beta$ is proportional to the square root of the angular velocity $\omega$.

Let $k$ be a positive proportionality constant:

$$\beta = k\sqrt{\omega}$$

Since $\beta$ is deceleration, the rate of change of angular velocity with respect to time $\left(\dfrac{d\omega}{dt}\right)$ is negative:

$$\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = -k\sqrt{\omega} \rightarrow \dfrac{d\omega}{\sqrt{\omega}} = -k\,dt$$

At $t = 0$, the initial angular velocity is $\omega_0$.

The body comes to rest $(\omega = 0)$ at a final time $t = t_0$:

$$\int\limits_{\omega_0}^{0} \omega^{-1/2}\, d\omega = \int\limits_{0}^{t_0} -k\, dt$$

Integrating both sides:

$$\left[2\sqrt{\omega}\right]_{\omega_0}^{0} = -k[t]_{0}^{t_0}$$

$$0 - 2\sqrt{\omega_0} = -k t_0$$

$$t_0 = \dfrac{2\sqrt{\omega_0}}{k}$$

The angular deceleration can also be written as $\beta = -\omega\, \dfrac{d\omega}{d\theta}$.

$$\omega\, \dfrac{d\omega}{d\theta} = -k\sqrt{\omega}$$

Assuming $\omega \ne 0$ before stopping,

$$\sqrt{\omega}\, d\omega = -k\, d\theta$$

At the initial moment, the angular displacement $\theta = 0$ when $\omega = \omega_0$.

When the body comes to rest, $\theta = \theta_{\text{total}}$ when $\omega = 0$:

$$\int\limits_{\omega_0}^{0} \omega^{1/2}\, d\omega = -k \int\limits_{0}^{\theta_{\text{total}}} d\theta$$

Integrating both sides:

$$\left[\dfrac{2}{3}\omega^{3/2}\right]_{\omega_0}^{0} = -k\, \theta_{\text{total}}$$

$0-\frac{2}{3} \omega_0^{3 / 2}=-\mathrm{k} \theta_{\text {total }}$

$$ \theta_{\text {total }}=\frac{2 \omega_0^{3 / 2}}{3 \mathrm{k}} $$

The mean angular velocity averaged over the total time of rotation is

$$ \omega_{\text {mean }}=\frac{\theta_{\text {total }}}{t_{\text {total }}}=\frac{\theta_{\text {total }}}{t_0} $$

$$\omega_{\text{mean}} = \frac{\left(\frac{2\omega_0^{3/2}}{3k}\right)}{\left(\frac{2\sqrt{\omega_0}}{k}\right)}$$

$$\omega_{\text{mean}} = \frac{\omega_0^{3/2}}{3\sqrt{\omega_0}} = \frac{\omega_0}{3}$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{\omega_0}{n} = \frac{\omega_0}{3} \Rightarrow n = 3$$

Therefore, the value of n is 3.

subject: Physics
q: A particle moves clockwise in a circle of radius 1 m with centre at $(x, y)=(1 \mathrm{~m}, 0)$ It starts at rest at the origin at time $t=0$ Its speed increases at the constant rate of $\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^2$. If the net acceleration at $t= 2 \sec$ is $\frac{\pi}{2} \sqrt{1+N \pi^2}$ then what is the value of $N$ ?
question: A particle moves clockwise in a circle of radius 1 m with centre at $(x, y)=(1 \mathrm{~m}, 0)$ It starts at rest at the origin at time $t=0$ Its speed increases at the constant rate of $\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^2$. If the net acceleration at $t= 2 \sec$ is $\frac{\pi}{2} \sqrt{1+N \pi^2}$ then what is the value of $N$ ?
solution:
The rate at which the speed of a particle increases along its path is its tangential acceleration ($a_t$).

$$a_t = \frac{\pi}{2}\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$$

Tangential acceleration is defined as the derivative of speed with respect to time:

$$a_t = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{\pi}{2} \rightarrow dv = \frac{\pi}{2}\,dt$$

As the particle started from rest ($v = 0$ at $t = 0$):

$$\int\limits_{0}^{v} v\,dv = \int\limits_{0}^{t} \frac{\pi}{2}\,dt$$

$$v(t) = \frac{\pi}{2}t$$

At $t = 2\ \mathrm{s}$:

$$v = \frac{\pi}{2} \times 2 = \pi\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

When a particle moves in a circular path of radius R, it experiences a centripetal acceleration ($a_c$) directed toward the centre due to the changing direction of its velocity.

$$ \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{R}} $$

The radius of circle is given $\mathrm{R}=1 \mathrm{~m}$ and speed at $\mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{~s}$ is $\mathrm{v}=\pi \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.

$$ \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\pi^2}{1}=\pi^2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2 $$

Since the tangential acceleration vector and centripetal acceleration vector are always mutually perpendicular ( $\vec{a}_t \perp \vec{a}_c=0$ ), the magnitude of the net acceleration ( $a_{n e t}$ ) is,

$$a_{\text{net}} = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_c^2}$$

$$a_{\text{net}} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^2 + (\pi^2)^2}$$

$$a_{\text{net}} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^2 \left(1 + \frac{\pi^4}{\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^2}\right)}$$

$$a_{\text{net}} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^2 \left(1 + \frac{\pi^4}{\pi^2/4}\right)}$$

$$a_{\text{net}} = \frac{\pi}{2}\sqrt{1 + 4\pi^2} = \frac{\pi}{2}\sqrt{1 + N\pi^2}$$

$$\Rightarrow n = 4$$

Therefore, the value of n is 4.

subject: Physics
q: Two particles $A$ and $B$ move anticlockwise with the same speed $v$ in a circle of radius $R$ and are diametrically opposite to each other. At $\mathbf{t}=\mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{A}$ is imparted a tangential acceleration of constant magnitude $a_t=\frac{72 v^2}{25 \pi R}$. If the time in which $A$ collides with $B$ is $\frac{5 \pi R}{N_1 v}$ the angle traced by $A$ during this time is $\frac{11 \pi}{N_2}$, its angular velocity is $\frac{17 v}{N_3 R}$ and radial acceleration at the time of collision is $\frac{289 v^2}{5 R N_4}$. Then calculate the value of $\mathbf{N}_1+\mathbf{N}_2+\mathbf{N}_3+\mathbf{N}_4$.
question: Two particles $A$ and $B$ move anticlockwise with the same speed $v$ in a circle of radius $R$ and are diametrically opposite to each other. At $\mathbf{t}=\mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{A}$ is imparted a tangential acceleration of constant magnitude $a_t=\frac{72 v^2}{25 \pi R}$. If the time in which $A$ collides with $B$ is $\frac{5 \pi R}{N_1 v}$ the angle traced by $A$ during this time is $\frac{11 \pi}{N_2}$, its angular velocity is $\frac{17 v}{N_3 R}$ and radial acceleration at the time of collision is $\frac{289 v^2}{5 R N_4}$. Then calculate the value of $\mathbf{N}_1+\mathbf{N}_2+\mathbf{N}_3+\mathbf{N}_4$.
solution:
Initially, particles A and B are diametrically opposite, so the initial angular separation between them is $\Delta \theta_0= \pi \mathrm{rad}$.

Both are moving anticlockwise with the same initial speed v , so their initial angular velocities are equal: $\omega_{\mathrm{A}}= \omega_B=\frac{v}{R}$. Thus, their initial relative angular velocity is zero.

At $t = 0$, particle A receives a constant tangential acceleration $a_t$.

$$a_t = R\alpha_A \rightarrow \alpha_A = \frac{a_t}{R} = \frac{72v^2}{25\pi R^2}$$

Since particle B continues moving at a constant speed $v$, its angular acceleration is $\alpha_B = 0$.

The relative angular acceleration of A with respect to B is:

$$\alpha_{\mathrm{rel}} = \alpha_A - \alpha_B = \frac{72v^2}{25\pi R^2}$$

Using the second equation of rotational motion for relative angular displacement

$$\Delta \theta = \omega_{\mathrm{rel},0} t + \frac{1}{2}\alpha_{\mathrm{rel}} t^2$$

For A to catch up and collide with B, it must cover the initial angular separation of $\pi$ radians:

$$\pi = 0 + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{72v^2}{25\pi R^2}\right)t_c^2$$

$$\pi = \frac{36v^2}{25\pi R^2} t_c^2 \Rightarrow t_c^2 = \frac{25\pi^2 R^2}{36v^2}$$

$$t_c = \frac{5\pi R}{6v} = \frac{5\pi R}{N_1 v} \Rightarrow N_1 = 6$$

The angular distance covered by particle A during this time depends on its own initial angular velocity $\omega_A = \frac{v}{R}$ and its angular acceleration $\alpha_A$:

$$\theta_A = \omega_A t_c + \frac{1}{2}\alpha_A t_c^2$$

Substituting $t_c = \frac{5\pi R}{6v}$ and $\alpha_A = \frac{72v^2}{25\pi R^2}$:

$$ \theta_A = \left(\frac{v}{R}\right)\left(\frac{5\pi R}{6v}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{72v^2}{25\pi R^2}\right)\left(\frac{25\pi^2 R^2}{36v^2}\right) $$

$$ \theta_A = \frac{5\pi}{6} + \pi = \frac{11\pi}{6} = \frac{11\pi}{N_2} \Rightarrow N_2 = 6 $$

Using the first equation of rotational motion $\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha\, t$:

$$ \omega_A = \frac{v}{R} + \left(\frac{72v^2}{25\pi R^2}\right)\left(\frac{5\pi R}{6v}\right) $$

$$ \omega_A = \frac{v}{R} + \frac{\left(\frac{72}{6}\right)\left(\frac{5}{25}\right)v}{R} = \frac{v}{R} + 12 \times \frac{1v}{5R} = \frac{17v}{5R} $$

$$ \omega_A = \frac{17v}{5R} = \frac{17v}{N_3R} \Rightarrow N_3 = 5 $$

The radial acceleration of a particle moving in a circle of radius $R$ with angular velocity $\omega$ is $a_r = \omega^2 R$.

$$ a_r = \left(\frac{17v}{5R}\right)^2 R = \frac{289v^2}{25R^2} R = \frac{289v^2}{25R} = \frac{289v^2}{5RN_4} $$

$$ \Rightarrow N_4 = 5 $$

Hence, $N_1 + N_2 + N_3 + N_4 = 6 + 6 + 5 + 5 = 22$. Thus, the correct answer is 22.

q: A car goes on a horizontal circular road of radius $\mathbf{R}=\sqrt{\mathbf{2 7}}$ meter, the speed increasing at a constant rate $\frac{d v}{d t}=a=1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ starting from rest. The friction coefficient between the road and the tyre is $\mu=0.2$. Find the time at which the car will skid.
question: A car goes on a horizontal circular road of radius $\mathbf{R}=\sqrt{\mathbf{2 7}}$ meter, the speed increasing at a constant rate $\frac{d v}{d t}=a=1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ starting from rest. The friction coefficient between the road and the tyre is $\mu=0.2$. Find the time at which the car will skid.
solution:
When a car moves on a horizontal circular track, the frictional force between the tires and the road provides the necessary total acceleration.

The maximum static friction force by the road on tyre is,

$$ \mathrm{f}_{\max }=\mu \mathrm{N} $$

Since the road is horizontal, the normal force N balances the weight of the car $(\mathrm{mg})$ :

$$ \mathrm{N}=\mathrm{mg} \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}_{\max }=\mu \mathrm{mg} $$

The car will begin to skid the moment the required total net force ( $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{net}}$ ) exceeds this maximum friction force:

$$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{net}}=\mathrm{f}_{\max } \Rightarrow \mathrm{ma}_{\mathrm{net}}=\mu \mathrm{mg} \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{net}}=\mu \mathrm{g} $$

The rate of change of linear speed is the tangential acceleration $\left(\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{t}}\right)$.

$$ a_t=\frac{d v}{d t}=1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2 $$

The acceleration toward the centre due to the changing direction of velocity is centripetal acceleration $\left(\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{c}}\right)$,

$$ \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{R}} $$

The net acceleration vector of a car speeding up on a circular path is the vector sum of two mutually perpendicular components. So, the magnitude of the net acceleration is :

$$ a_{n e t}=\sqrt{a_t^2+a_c^2}=\sqrt{a^2+\left(\frac{v^2}{R}\right)^2} $$

The car started from rest $(\mathrm{v}=0$ at $\mathrm{t}=0)$ and with constant speed $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{t}}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$.

Using equation of motion, the final linear speed after time $t$ is,

$$ v=u+a t \Rightarrow v(t)=0+(1) t=t $$

So, the centripetal acceleration is $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\mathrm{t}^2}{\mathrm{R}}$

Set the net acceleration equal to the skidding acceleration $\mathrm{a}_{\text {net }}=\mu \mathrm{g}$ :

$$ \sqrt{a_t^2+a_c^2}=\mu g $$

$$\sqrt{1^2+\left(\frac{t^2}{R}\right)^2}=\mu g$$

$$1+\frac{t^4}{R^2}=(\mu g)^2$$

$$\frac{t^4}{R^2}=(\mu g)^2-1$$

$$t^4=R^2\left[(\mu g)^2-1\right]$$

$$(\mu g)^2==2^2=4$$

$$t^4=$$

$$t^4=(\sqrt{27})^{v}\times\left[(0.2\times 10)^2-1\right]$$

$$t^4=27\times[4-1]=27\times 3=81$$

$$t=(81)^{1/4}=3\ \text{sec}$$

Therefore, the car will begin to skid at $t=3$ seconds.

q: A block of mass $m=1 \mathrm{~kg}$ moves on a horizontal circle against the wall of a cylindrical room of radius $\mathbf{R}=\mathbf{2} \sqrt{\mathbf{2}} \mathbf{m}$. The floor of the room on which the block moves is smooth but the friction coefficient between the wall and the block is $\mu=1$ The block is given an initial speed $v_0$. If speed at an instant is $v=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ then calculate resultant acceleration of block in $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ at that instant
question: A block of mass $m=1 \mathrm{~kg}$ moves on a horizontal circle against the wall of a cylindrical room of radius $\mathbf{R}=\mathbf{2} \sqrt{\mathbf{2}} \mathbf{m}$. The floor of the room on which the block moves is smooth but the friction coefficient between the wall and the block is $\mu=1$ The block is given an initial speed $v_0$. If speed at an instant is $v=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ then calculate resultant acceleration of block in $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ at that instant
solution:
The vertical wall pushes horizontally inward on the block to keep it moving in a circle. This normal force ( N ) provides the necessary centripetal acceleration.

Since the block slides along the wall, kinetic friction acts opposite to the direction of its tangential velocity vector.

The normal force from the wall is the only force directed toward the centre of the circular path.

From Newton's Second Law ($F_{\text{net}} = ma$):

$$N = m\,a_c = \frac{mv^2}{R}$$

Therefore, the radial acceleration component is:

$$a_c = \frac{v^2}{R}$$

The kinetic friction force acts in the tangential direction, opposing the motion.

The magnitude of kinetic friction is:

$$f_k = \mu N$$

Substituting expression for the normal force ($N = \frac{mv^2}{R}$):

$$f_k = \mu \left(\frac{mv^2}{R}\right)$$

This friction force causes a tangential deceleration.

Using Newton's Second Law along the tangential path ($f_k = m\,|a_t|$):

$$m\,|a_t| = \frac{\mu mv^2}{R} \rightarrow |a_t| = \frac{\mu v^2}{R}$$

The speed at particular instant is $v = 2\text{m/s}$, the radius of circle is $R = 2\sqrt{2}\ \text{m}$ and $\mu = 1$.

$$a_c = \frac{2^2}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2}\text{m/s}^2$$

$$|a_t| = \frac{1 \times 2^2}{2\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2}\ \text{m/s}^2$$

The radial acceleration vector points towards the centre of the circle and the tangential acceleration vector points along the path's tangent, they are perpendicular to each other ($\vec{a_c} \perp \vec{a_t} = 0$).

The magnitude of the total resultant acceleration is:

$$a_{\text{net}} = \sqrt{a_c^2 + a_t^2}$$

$$a_{\text{net}} = \sqrt{(\sqrt{2})^2 + (\sqrt{2})^2}$$

$$a_{\text{net}} = \sqrt{2 + 2} = \sqrt{4} = 2\ \text{m/s}^2$$

Therefore, the magnitude of the total acceleration of block is $2\ \text{m/s}^2$.
